title: Lou Donaldson

infobox.value: Louis Andrew Donaldson Jr.  (1926-11-01)November 1, 1926  [Badin, North Carolina](/wiki/Badin,_North_Carolina "Badin, North Carolina"), U.S.
November 9, 2024(2024-11-09) (aged 98)  [Daytona Beach, Florida](/wiki/Daytona_Beach,_Florida "Daytona Beach, Florida"), U.S.
[North Carolina A&T](/wiki/North_Carolina_A%26T_State_University "North Carolina A&T State University")
* [Bebop](/wiki/Bebop "Bebop") * [hard bop](/wiki/Hard_bop "Hard bop") * [jazz blues](/wiki/Jazz_blues "Jazz blues") * [soul jazz](/wiki/Soul_jazz "Soul jazz")
* Bandleader * composer * saxophonist
[Alto saxophone](/wiki/Alto_saxophone "Alto saxophone")
[loudonaldson.com](http://loudonaldson.com)

(November 1, 1926 – November 9, 2024) was an American jazz [alto saxophonist](/wiki/Alto_saxophone "Alto saxophone"). He was best known for his soulful, bluesy approach to playing the alto saxophone, although in his formative years he was heavily influenced by [Charlie Parker](/wiki/Charlie_Parker "Charlie Parker"), as were many during the [bebop](/wiki/Bebop "Bebop") era.[[1]](#cite_note-ALLMUSIC-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American jazz saxophonist (1926–2024)

| Lou Donaldson | |

| [Donaldson in 2015](/wiki/File:Lou_Donaldson_Quartet_2015_(20259143008).jpg "Donaldson in 2015") Donaldson in 2015 | |
| Background information | |

| Genres | * [Bebop](/wiki/Bebop "Bebop") * [hard bop](/wiki/Hard_bop "Hard bop") * [jazz blues](/wiki/Jazz_blues "Jazz blues") * [soul jazz](/wiki/Soul_jazz "Soul jazz") |

Musical artist

**Louis Andrew Donaldson Jr.** (November 1, 1926 – November 9, 2024) was an American jazz [alto saxophonist](/wiki/Alto_saxophone "Alto saxophone"). He was best known for his soulful, bluesy approach to playing the alto saxophone, although in his formative years he was heavily influenced by [Charlie Parker](/wiki/Charlie_Parker "Charlie Parker"), as were many during the [bebop](/wiki/Bebop "Bebop") era.[[1]](#cite_note-ALLMUSIC-1)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lou_Donaldson&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Donaldson was born in [Badin, North Carolina](/wiki/Badin,_North_Carolina "Badin, North Carolina"), on November 1, 1926.[[2]](#cite_note-Mathieson2012-2)[[1]](#cite_note-ALLMUSIC-1) He attended [North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University](/wiki/North_Carolina_Agricultural_and_Technical_State_University "North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University") in [Greensboro](/wiki/Greensboro,_North_Carolina "Greensboro, North Carolina"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) in the early 1940s. He enlisted in the U.S. Navy during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and was trained at the Great Lakes bases in Chicago where he was introduced to bop music in the lively club scene.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lou_Donaldson&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Lou_Donaldson_1984.jpg/250px-Lou_Donaldson_1984.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lou_Donaldson_1984.jpg)

Lou Donaldson at VIS club, Divisadero Street, San Francisco in June 1984

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Lou_Donaldson_DSC0004a.jpg/250px-Lou_Donaldson_DSC0004a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lou_Donaldson_DSC0004a.jpg)

Donaldson in Buffalo, New York

At the war's conclusion, Donaldson returned to Greensboro, where he worked club dates with the Rhythm Vets, a combo composed of A and T students who had served in the U.S. Navy. The band recorded the soundtrack to a musical comedy featurette, *[Pitch a Boogie Woogie](/wiki/Pitch_a_Boogie_Woogie "Pitch a Boogie Woogie")*, in Greenville, North Carolina, in the summer of 1947. The movie had a limited run at black audience theatres in 1948 but its production company, Lord-Warner Pictures, folded and never made another film. *Pitch a Boogie Woogie* was restored by the [American Film Institute](/wiki/American_Film_Institute "American Film Institute") in 1985 and re-premiered on the campus of East Carolina University in Greenville the following year. Donaldson and the surviving members of the Vets performed a reunion concert after the film's showing. In the documentary made on *Pitch* by UNC-TV, *Boogie in Black and White*,[[4]](#cite_note-4) Donaldson and his musical cohorts recall the film's making—he originally believed that he had played clarinet on the soundtrack. A short piece of concert footage from a gig in Fayetteville, North Carolina, is included in the documentary.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Donaldson's first jazz recordings were with bop musicians [Milt Jackson](/wiki/Milt_Jackson "Milt Jackson") and [Thelonious Monk](/wiki/Thelonious_Monk "Thelonious Monk") in 1952,[[6]](#cite_note-6) and he participated in several small groups with other prominent jazz musicians such as trumpeter [Blue Mitchell](/wiki/Blue_Mitchell "Blue Mitchell"), pianist [Horace Silver](/wiki/Horace_Silver "Horace Silver"), and drummer [Art Blakey](/wiki/Art_Blakey "Art Blakey").[[1]](#cite_note-ALLMUSIC-1) In 1953, he also recorded sessions with the trumpeter [Clifford Brown](/wiki/Clifford_Brown "Clifford Brown"), and with [Philly Joe Jones](/wiki/Philly_Joe_Jones "Philly Joe Jones"). He was a member of Art Blakey's Quintet for the [hard bop](/wiki/Hard_bop "Hard bop") recording sessions at [Birdland](/wiki/Birdland_(New_York_jazz_club) "Birdland (New York jazz club)") on February 21, 1954, which would yield the *[A Night at Birdland](/wiki/A_Night_at_Birdland_Vol._1 "A Night at Birdland Vol. 1")* albums for [Blue Note Records](/wiki/Blue_Note_Records "Blue Note Records").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

He was inducted into the [North Carolina Music Hall of Fame](/wiki/North_Carolina_Music_Hall_of_Fame "North Carolina Music Hall of Fame") on October 11, 2012.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Also in 2012, he was named a NEA Jazz Master by the [National Endowment for the Arts](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_the_Arts "National Endowment for the Arts").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Retirement and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lou_Donaldson&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Retirement and death")]

In 2018, Donaldson declared himself retired, having performed his final shows in 2017. On November 2, 2021, he made a public appearance at a 95th birthday tribute show at [Dizzy's Club](/wiki/Jazz_at_Lincoln_Center "Jazz at Lincoln Center") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). He appeared at his 96th and 97th birthday tribute shows in 2022 and 2023, but opted not to travel to New York City for his 98th birthday celebration, due to a bout of pneumonia.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Shortly afterward, he died from pneumonia at a hospital in [Daytona Beach, Florida](/wiki/Daytona_Beach,_Florida "Daytona Beach, Florida"), on November 9, 2024.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lou_Donaldson&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Discography")]

Main article: [Lou Donaldson discography](/wiki/Lou_Donaldson_discography "Lou Donaldson discography")

["Lou Donaldson, saxophonist who helped shape jazz, dies at 98"](https://www.washingtonpost.com/obituaries/2024/11/11/lou-donaldson-jazz-saxophonist-dead/). *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")*. Retrieved November 11, 2024.
